summary:
The SSI rate for clean-contaminated wounds typically falls between 0.5%-3%, supporting an estimate close to but not exceeding <10%.

The patient has a clean-contaminated incision which increases their risk of developing postoperative complications such as SSIs compared to Class II or III contamination levels; however, it is still relatively low due to minimal tissue invasion and no direct fecal/peritoneal contact during surgery. To reduce risks further would require adherence to strict sterile techniques throughout all aspects of pre-, intra- and postsurgical care procedures along with appropriate prophylactic antibiotics if indicated based upon institutional guidelines and local infection control policies. Summary: Clean-contaminated wounds have a typical SSI rate ranging from 0.5% to 3%; thus, estimating less than 10% aligns well with most published data while acknowledging individual variations depending on specific circumstances. Proper sterilization practices combined with judicious antibiotic use can help minimize potential issues associated with these types of injuries.
